target.name: Chartreusin
target.motivation: Chartreusin-type APP glycosides exhibit promising antitumoral activities and antibiotic activities. The goal is to establish robust and efficient chemical synthesis approaches to overcome limitations of biosynthesis and chemobiosynthesis, enabling access to diverse and ample amounts of chartreusin derivatives for pharmaceutical development.

success_criteria.free_text: Successful establishment of the first chemical synthesis strategy to secure chartreusin derivatives, whereby the collective and highly efficient syntheses of chartreusin, D329C, and elsamicins A and B were achieved. The protocols should feature two complementary routes to secure chartarin 10-O-monosaccharide glycosides and highly stereoselective construction of difficult glycosidic linkages.

key_disconnections.reasoning: The phenolic glycosidic linkage appending the sugar residues to the chartarin core is intractable. Yu glycosylation is chosen due to mild reaction conditions and fully testified synthetic potential.
Hauser–Kraus annulation is chosen for aglycone skeleton formation due to mild reaction conditions and fully testified synthetic potential.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: Two complementary routes were designed to secure chartarin 10-O-monosaccharide glycosides. Route a involves Hauser–Kraus annulation between glycosylated phthalide B and coumarin C, constructing the challenging phenolic glycosidic linkage prior to the complete chartarin aglycone core. Route b explores direct glycosylation of chartarin acceptor E with monosaccharide Yu donor D to enhance convergence. Both routes aim to address the difficulties of chartarin as a glycosylation acceptor (low solubility, electron-deficient core, intramolecular H-bond) and the challenging phenolic glycosidic linkage, especially with rare 3-C-methyl-branched sugars.

thought.background: Chartreusin-type APP glycosides are challenging targets due to their intricate chemical structures, including the unusual benzonaphthopyranone aglycone with a bislactone scaffold (chartarin), the intractable phenolic glycosidic linkage, and the presence of rare 3-C-methyl-branched sugars and 1,2-cis glycosidic linkages. Prior biosynthesis and chemobiosynthesis methods have scalability issues.
thought.gap: No chemical total synthesis of chartreusin derivatives has been reported. Key challenges include preparing chartarin derivatives suitable for C10-OH glycosylation, overcoming chartarin's unfavorable properties as a glycosylation acceptor (low solubility, electron-deficient core, H-bonding), and achieving stereoselective formation of phenolic and intra-sugar-chain glycosidic linkages, especially with 3-C-methyl-branched sugars.
thought.decision: To address these challenges, a chemical synthesis strategy will be developed. Retrosynthetically, chartreusin and its analogues will be traced back to a monosaccharide glycoside intermediate (A), which can be assembled via two routes: Route a (Hauser–Kraus annulation between glycosylated phthalide B and coumarin C) and Route b (direct Yu glycosylation of chartarin acceptor E with monosaccharide Yu donor D). This dual approach aims to balance flexibility and convergence.

evidence.text_span: Being aware of the origins of challenges in the chemical synthesis of chartreusin-type APP glycosides, we realized that the successful development of a chemical synthesis strategy would highly rely on efficient synthesis of chartarin 10-Omonosaccharide glycoside. As such, retrosynthetically, chartreusin (1), D329C (2), and elsamicins A (3) and B (4) could be traced back to monosaccharide glycoside A (Fig. 2), which in turn could be assembled by two different routes. Route a, involving Hauser–Kraus annulation between glycosylated phthalide B and coumarin C, was characterized by arranging the construction of the challenging phenolic glycosidic linkage prior to the fabrication of the complete chartarin aglycone core, so as to bypass the direct construction of the thorny chartarin 10-O-glycosidic linkage and improve the flexibility of the synthetic approach as well. Meanwhile, encouraged by the significant advances in carbohydrate chemistry,28 direct glycosylation of chartarin acceptor E with monosaccharide Yu donor D would also be explored in order to enhance the convergence of the synthetic method (route b). The choice of the Hauser– Kraus reaction for aglycone skeleton formation and the Yu donor29 for glycosidic linkage construction is due to the mild reaction conditions and fully testified synthetic potential.

thought.background: Route a, involving glycosylated phthalide intermediates, is being pursued first. The initial step is to synthesize phenol glycoside 7 from peracetylated β-galactoside 5 and 2-methyl-3-methoxycarbonyl phenol 6.
thought.gap: The formation of the initial glycosidic linkage needs to be efficient and high yielding.
thought.decision: To form phenol glycoside 7, peracetylated β-galactoside 5 will be condensed with 2-methyl-3-methoxycarbonyl phenol 6 under BF3·Et2O catalysis, expecting a high yield.

outcome.observation: Condensation delivered phenol glycoside 7 in 95% yield.

evidence.text_span: The endeavor commenced with the synthesis of glycosylated phthalide intermediates (Scheme 1A). Condensation between peracetylated β-galactoside 5 with 2-methyl-3- methoxycarbonyl phenol 6 under the effect of BF3·Et2O delivered phenol glycoside 7 (95%)

thought.background: Phenol glycoside 7 has been successfully synthesized. The next step is to convert 7 to iodide 8 through a sequence of deacetylation, acetonide formation, open-chain ketal cleavage, and Appel reaction.
thought.gap: This multi-step sequence needs to be efficient to provide iodide 8 for subsequent deiodination.
thought.decision: To convert 7 to 8, a four-step sequence will be performed: deacetylation with NaOMe, acetonide formation with 2,2-DMP/TsOH, open-chain ketal cleavage with PPTS, and Appel reaction with I2/Ph3P/imidazole, expecting an overall good yield.

outcome.observation: Compound 7 was converted to 8 in 89% yield over 4 steps.

evidence.text_span: which was then converted to 8 via a sequence of deacetylation, acetonide formation, open-chain ketal cleavage, and Appel reaction (89%, 4 steps).

thought.background: Iodide 8 has been synthesized. The next step is deiodination to generate phenyl D-fucoside 9.
thought.gap: Efficient removal of the iodine atom is required.
thought.decision: To generate phenyl D-fucoside 9, iodide 8 will undergo deiodination under radical conditions using AIBN and nBu3SnH in toluene, expecting a smooth reaction.

outcome.observation: Iodide 8 proceeded deiodination smoothly to generate phenyl D-fucoside 9 in 93% yield.

evidence.text_span: Under radical conditions, iodide 8 proceeded deiodination smoothly to generate phenyl D-fucoside 9 (93%).

thought.background: Phenyl D-fucoside 9 has been obtained. The next step is selective bromination of the methyl group attached to the phenyl ring, followed by re-isopropylidenation.
thought.gap: Selective bromination is required, and the isopropylidenyl protecting group might be partially cleaved under bromination conditions.
thought.decision: To obtain bromide 10, selective bromination of 9 will be carried out under UV irradiation with NBS and AIBN at 0 °C. Due to potential partial cleavage of the isopropylidenyl group, a subsequent re-isopropylidenation step with 2,2-DMP/TsOH will be performed.

outcome.observation: Selective bromination of the methyl group was carried out. Although mild conditions were adopted, the isopropylidenyl protecting group was partially cleaved, necessitating re-isopropylidenation to afford bromide 10 in 82% yield over 2 steps.

evidence.text_span: Selective bromination of the methyl group attached to the phenyl ring was subsequently carried out under UV irradiation. Although mild conditions were adopted (0 °C), the isopropylidenyl protecting group in 9 was partially cleaved. Thus, re-isopropylidenation was needed to afford bromide 10 (82%, 2 steps).

thought.background: Bromide 10 has been synthesized. The goal is to convert it to fucosylated phthalide 11, an inseparable mixture of diastereomers.
thought.gap: This conversion requires a sequence of modified Kornblum oxidation, o-trimethylsilyl cyanohydrin formation, and acid-mediated lactonization.
thought.decision: To furnish fucosylated phthalide 11, intermediate 10 will be subjected to modified Kornblum oxidation (NMO, 4A MS), followed by Et3N-catalyzed o-trimethylsilyl cyanohydrin formation (TMSCN, Et3N), and finally acid-mediated lactonization (HOAc), expecting a good yield over these steps.

outcome.observation: Intermediate 10 was converted to fucosylated phthalide 11 in 75% yield over 2 steps, as an inseparable mixture of diastereomers.

evidence.text_span: Afterwards, intermediate 10 was subjected successively to modified Kornblum oxidation,31 Et3N·-catalyzed o-trimethylsilyl cyanohydrin formation,32 and acid-mediated lactonization,33 to furnish fucosylated phthalide 11 (75%, 2 steps) as an inseparable mixture of diastereomers.

thought.background: Synthesis of phthalide with 3-C-methyl-branched fucosyl residue is initiated with fucosyl derivative 12. The first step is benzylation of 12.
thought.gap: Efficient and high-yielding benzylation is required.
thought.decision: To provide 13, fucosyl derivative 12 will be benzylated using NaH and BnBr in DMF, expecting a high yield.

outcome.observation: Fucosyl derivative 12 was benzylated to provide 13 in 98% yield.

evidence.text_span: Synthesis of phthalide with 3-C-methyl-branched fucosyl residue started with fucosyl derivative 12 (Scheme 1B), which was benzylated to provide 13 (98%).

thought.background: Benzylated compound 13 has been obtained. The next step is acid-mediated de-isopropylidenation to convert 13 to diol 14.
thought.gap: Efficient and high-yielding deprotection is required.
thought.decision: To convert 13 to diol 14, acid-mediated de-isopropylidenation will be performed using 80% HOAc at 80 °C, expecting a high yield.

outcome.observation: Acid-mediated de-isopropylidenation converted 13 to diol 14 in 99% yield.

evidence.text_span: Acid-mediated de-isopropylidenation converted 13 to diol 14 (99%)

thought.background: Diol 14 has been obtained. The next step is selective blocking of the equatorial C3-OH as a silyl ether to afford 15.
thought.gap: Regioselective protection of one hydroxyl group in the presence of another is required.
thought.decision: To afford 15, the equatorial C3-OH of 14 will be selectively blocked as a silyl ether using TBSCl and imidazole, expecting a good yield.

outcome.observation: Equatorial C3-OH was selectively blocked as a silyl ether to afford 15 in 92% yield.

evidence.text_span: whose equatorial C3-OH was then selectively blocked as a silyl ether to afford 15 (92%).

thought.background: Compound 15 has been obtained. The next step is to protect the remaining axial C4-OH with a benzyl group, followed by removal of the temporary C3-OTBS group, delivering alcohol intermediate 16.
thought.gap: This two-step sequence needs to be efficient to prepare 16 for 3-C-methyl branch installation.
thought.decision: To deliver alcohol intermediate 16, the remaining axial C4-OH of 15 will be protected with a benzyl group (NaH, BnBr in DMF), and then the temporary C3-OTBS will be removed (TBAF in THF), expecting a good overall yield.

outcome.observation: After the remaining axial C4-OH was protected with a benzyl group, the temporary C3-OTBS was removed, delivering the alcohol intermediate 16 in 77% yield over 2 steps.

evidence.text_span: After the remaining axial C4-OH was protected with a benzyl group, the temporary C3-OTBS was removed, delivering the alcohol intermediate 16 (77%, 2 steps) primed for 3-Cmethyl branch installation.

thought.background: Alcohol intermediate 16 is primed for 3-C-methyl branch installation. This involves oxidation of 16, followed by methyl Grignard reagent addition to the resulting ketone group to provide tertiary alcohol 18 via 17.
thought.gap: The oxidation and subsequent Grignard addition need to be efficient and stereoselective.
thought.decision: To provide tertiary alcohol 18 via 17, 16 will be oxidized using DMP in DCM, and then the resulting ketone will be treated with MeMgBr in THF at -78 °C to rt, expecting a good overall yield and stereoselective addition.

outcome.observation: Oxidation of 16 was followed by methyl Grignard reagent addition to the resulting ketone group to provide tertiary alcohol 18 via 17 in 88% yield over 2 steps. The addition took place axially, disposing the nascent tertiary OH in the equatorial position exclusively.

evidence.text_span: Oxidation of 16 was followed by methyl Grignard reagent addition to the resulting ketone group to provide tertiary alcohol 18 via 17 (88%, 2 steps). The addition of methyl Grignard reagent to the C3-ketone of 17 took place axially disposing the nascent tertiary OH in the equatorial position exclusively, which was assigned by the NOE experiment.34

thought.background: Tertiary alcohol 18 has been obtained. The next step is hydrogenolysis to cleave the two benzyl groups, coupled with regioselective C2-OH silylation and C4-OH picoloylation to furnish 19.
thought.gap: This multi-step sequence needs to be efficient and regioselective to prepare 19, which is designed to steer ensuing glycosylation stereoselectively.
thought.decision: To furnish 19, 18 will undergo hydrogenolysis (H2, Pd/C, HOAc, THF/MeOH), followed by regioselective C2-OH silylation (TBSCl, imidazole, DMF), and C4-OH picoloylation (PicoOH, EDCI, DMAP, DIPEA, DCM), expecting a good overall yield.

outcome.observation: Hydrogenolysis of 18 coupled with regioselective C2-OH silylation and C4-OH picoloylation furnished 19 in 68% yield over 3 steps. The installation of TBS and Pico groups was aimed at steering the ensuing glycosylation to proceed β-stereoselectively.

evidence.text_span: Hydrogenolysis of 18 to cleave the two benzyl groups coupled with regioselective C2-OH silylation and C4-OH picoloylation furnished 19 (68%, 3 steps). The installation of TBS and Pico groups at C2 and C4 OHs, respectively, was aimed at steering the ensuing glycosylation to proceed β-stereoselectively both by steric hindrance (from the TBS group) and by the HAD strategy (from the Pico group).35

thought.background: Compound 19 has been obtained, designed to steer β-stereoselective glycosylation. The next step is to convert 19 to Yu donor 20 β-stereoselectively.
thought.gap: This requires oxidative cleavage of the anomeric p-methoxyphenyl group to yield the hemiacetal intermediate, followed by o-cyclopropylethynylbenzoylation under dehydrative conditions.
thought.decision: To generate Yu donor 20 β-stereoselectively, 19 will undergo oxidative cleavage of the anomeric MP group (CAN in PhMe/MeCN/H2O), followed by o-cyclopropylethynylbenzoylation (ABzOH, EDCI, DMAP, DIPEA in DCM), expecting a good overall yield and β-stereoselectivity.

outcome.observation: Oxidative cleavage of the anomeric p-methoxyphenyl group yielded the hemiacetal intermediate, which was further subjected to o-cyclopropylethynylbenzoylation under dehydrative conditions to generate Yu donor 20 β-stereoselectively in 75% yield over 2 steps.

evidence.text_span: Oxidative cleavage of the anomeric p-methoxyphenyl group yielded the hemiacetal intermediate, which was further subjected to o-cyclopropylethynylbenzoylation under dehydrative conditions to generate Yu donor 20 b-stereoselectively (75%, 2 steps).

thought.background: Yu donor 20 has been synthesized. The next step is to couple 20 with 2-methyl-3-methoxycarbonyl phenol 6.
thought.gap: The coupling needs to proceed smoothly and stereoselectively, especially given the challenges with 3-C-methyl fucosyl donors.
thought.decision: To achieve the coupling, 20 will be reacted with 6 under the effect of Au(I) complex (Ph3PAuNTf2) in DCM with 4A MS, expecting a stereoselective and efficient reaction.

outcome.observation: The coupling between 6 and the elaborately designed Yu donor 20 proceeded smoothly under the effect of Au(I) complex, providing 21 stereoselectively and efficiently in 76% yield (anomeric proton: 5.00 ppm, d, J = 8.0 Hz).

evidence.text_span: In sharp contrast to the results from the Kim group,23 and, pleasantly, the coupling between 6 and the elaborately designed Yu donor 20 proceeded smoothly under the effect of Au(I) complex providing 21 stereoselectively and efficiently (76%, for the anomeric proton: 5.00 ppm, d, J = 8.0 Hz).36

thought.background: Compound 21 has been obtained. Before constructing the cyanophthalide moiety, protecting group adjustments are needed.
thought.gap: This involves a sequence of Pico group removal, acetonization, and desilylation to give 22.
thought.decision: To give 22, 21 will undergo Pico group removal (Cu(OAc)2 in MeOH/DCM), followed by acetonization (2,2-DMP, p-TsOH), and desilylation (TBAF, HOAc in THF), expecting a good overall yield.

outcome.observation: Protecting group adjustments were conducted via a sequence of Pico group removal, acetonization, and desilylation to give 22 in 86% yield over 3 steps.

evidence.text_span: After completing the formation of the challenging glycosidic linkage, now the stage was set for the construction of the cyanophthalide moiety. Before this, protecting group adjustments were conducted via a sequence of Pico group removal, actonization, and desilylation to give 22 (86%, 3 steps).

thought.background: Compound 22 has been obtained. The final step in this route is to convert 22 to cyanophthalide 24 via intermediate 23, following a similar sequence used for the synthesis of 11 from 9.
thought.gap: This conversion requires selective bromination, modified Kornblum oxidation, o-trimethylsilyl cyanohydrin formation, and acid-mediated lactonization.
thought.decision: To convert 22 to cyanophthalide 24, it will be subjected to selective bromination (NBS, AIBN, UV), followed by modified Kornblum oxidation (NMO, 4A MS), o-trimethylsilyl cyanohydrin formation (TMSCN, Et3N), and acid-mediated lactonization (HOAc), expecting an efficient conversion.

outcome.observation: Following a similar sequence to that used for the synthesis of 11 from 9, 22 was converted to cyanophthalide 24 via intermediate 23 uneventfully in 88% yield over 3 steps.

evidence.text_span: Eventually, following a similar sequence to that used for the synthesis of 11 from 9, 22 was converted to cyanophthalide 24 via intermediate 23 uneventfully (88%, 3 steps).

thought.background: Phthalides 11 and 24, and coumarin C are in hand. The pivotal Hauser–Kraus annulation needs to be examined.
thought.gap: The annulation reaction is known to be highly steric hindrance sensitive, and complex glycosylated partners have only been reported once. Optimal conditions need to be identified.
thought.decision: To achieve the annulation, 11 will be reacted with coumarin C using tBuOLi as the base in THF at -78 °C to rt, aiming for the optimal result. A large excess of tBuOLi or prolonged reaction time will be avoided due to potential side reactions.

outcome.observation: After extensive trials, the optimal result for the annulation of 11 was obtained when tBuOLi (1.1 eq) was selected as the base, delivering 25 in 63% yield. A large excess of tBuOLi or prolonged reaction time was detrimental.

evidence.text_span: With phthalides 11 and 24 as well as coumarin C in hand, the pivotal Hauser–Kraus annulation was examined (Scheme 1C). Despite the high application frequency of the Hauser–Kraus reaction in total synthesis, annulations entailing complex glycosylated partners have only been reported once.40 After extensive trials (Table S2 in the ESI†), it was revealed that the annulation reaction was highly steric hindrance sensitive, and MOM or TBS protected 11 only gave low yields of the desired annulation products. With 11 as a substrate the optimal result was obtained when t BuOLi (1.1 eq) was selected as the base (25, 63%).34 A large excess of t BuOLi or prolonged reaction time was detrimental to the desired annulation.

thought.background: The Hauser–Kraus annulation of 11 was successful. Now, the more challenging annulation between 24 and C needs to be performed under the optimized conditions.
thought.gap: Ensuring a smooth reaction and respectable yield for the more challenging substrate 24 is crucial.
thought.decision: To achieve the annulation, 24 will be reacted with coumarin C using tBuOLi as the base in THF at -78 °C to rt, expecting a smooth reaction and good yield.

outcome.observation: Under the optimized conditions, the more challenging annulation between 24 and C proceeded smoothly, presenting 27 in a respectable 55% yield.

evidence.text_span: Under the optimized conditions, the more challenging annulation between 24 and C proceeded smoothly, presenting 27 in a respectable 55% yield.

thought.background: Compounds 25 and 27 have been obtained from the Hauser–Kraus annulation. The final step for route a is selective protection of the C6-OHs of 25 and 27 with the TBDPS group to afford 26 and 28, respectively.
thought.gap: Efficient and selective protection of the C6-OH is required to complete the synthesis of chartarin 10-O-monosaccharide glycosides.
thought.decision: To afford 26 and 28, selective protection of the C6-OHs of 25 and 27 will be performed using TBDPSCl and DBU in DCM, expecting good yields.

outcome.observation: Selective protection of the C6-OH of 25 with the TBDPS group afforded 26 in 51% yield.

evidence.text_span: Selective protection of the C6-OHs of 11 and 24 with the TBDPS group afforded 26 and 28, thus achieving the successful synthesis of chartarin 10-O-monosaccharide glycosides, the key intermediate for the chemical synthesis of chartreusin derivatives (51% and 74% yield, respectively).

thought.background: Compounds 25 and 27 have been obtained from the Hauser–Kraus annulation. The final step for route a is selective protection of the C6-OHs of 25 and 27 with the TBDPS group to afford 26 and 28, respectively.
thought.gap: Efficient and selective protection of the C6-OH is required to complete the synthesis of chartarin 10-O-monosaccharide glycosides.
thought.decision: To afford 26 and 28, selective protection of the C6-OHs of 25 and 27 will be performed using TBDPSCl and DBU in DCM, expecting good yields.

outcome.observation: Selective protection of the C6-OH of 27 with the TBDPS group afforded 28 in 74% yield.

evidence.text_span: Selective protection of the C6-OHs of 11 and 24 with the TBDPS group afforded 26 and 28, thus achieving the successful synthesis of chartarin 10-O-monosaccharide glycosides, the key intermediate for the chemical synthesis of chartreusin derivatives (51% and 74% yield, respectively).

thought.background: Route a has successfully yielded chartarin 10-O-monosaccharide glycosides 26 and 28. Now, route b, featuring direct glycosylation of chartarin-derivative acceptors, will be attempted. The first step is to prepare chartarin derivatives with ameliorated acceptor properties, starting with 29.
thought.gap: The C6-OH of 29 needs to be silylated to improve acceptor properties (electron-donating, bulkiness to distort planarity, enhance solubility).
thought.decision: To yield 30, silylation of C6-OH of 29 will be conducted under the effect of DBU/TBDPSCl in DCM, expecting a good yield and improved acceptor properties.

outcome.observation: Silylation of C6-OH of 29 was conducted under the effect of DBU/TBDPSCl to yield 30 in 91% yield. The choice of TBDPS was based on its electron-donating capability and bulkiness to ameliorate the electron-deficient nature and distort planarity of the aglycone.

evidence.text_span: Preparation of chartarin derivatives with ameliorated acceptor properties was explored first (Scheme 2). With 29 as the starting material, silylation of C6-OH was conducted under the effect of DBU/TBDPSCl to yield 30 (91%). The choice of TBDPS as the protecting group for C6-OH was based on the assumption that its electron-donating capability could ameliorate the electron-deficient nature of the dilactonized benzonaphthopyranone core of chartarin meanwhile, the considerable bulkiness of TBDPS was expected to distort the planarity of the aglycone and accordingly enhance the solubility in glycosylation media.

thought.background: Compound 30 has been obtained with improved acceptor properties. The next step is C10-OMOM cleavage to secure chartarin acceptor 31.
thought.gap: Efficient and high-yielding deprotection is required.
thought.decision: To secure chartarin acceptor 31, C10-OMOM cleavage will be performed using TMSBr in DCM at -20 °C, expecting a high yield.

outcome.observation: After C10-OMOM cleavage, chartarin acceptor 31 was secured in 95% yield. Indeed, 31 possesses reasonable solubility in dichloromethane.

evidence.text_span: After C10-OMOM cleavage,41 chartarin acceptor 31 was secured (95%, Scheme 2A). Indeed, 31 possesses reasonable solubility in dichloromethane, the preferred solvent for glycosylation reactions.

thought.background: Chartarin acceptor 31 is ready. Now, synthesis of donor 34 will commence with regioselective benzylation of 14 via in situ generated stannylene acetal.
thought.gap: Regioselective protection of one hydroxyl group in the presence of others is required.
thought.decision: To give 32, regioselective benzylation of 14 will be performed via in situ generated stannylene acetal (Bu2SnO, PhMe, reflux then TBAB, CsF, BnBr, PhMe, reflux), expecting a good yield.

outcome.observation: Regioselective benzylation of 14 via the in situ generated stannylene acetal gave 32 in 94% yield.

evidence.text_span: Synthesis of donor 34 commenced with regioselective benzylation of 14 via the in situ generated stannylene acetal to give 32 (94%),42

thought.background: Compound 32 has been obtained. The next step is to block the remaining free OH with a picoloyl group to afford 33.
thought.gap: Efficient and high-yielding picoloylation is required.
thought.decision: To afford 33, the remaining free OH of 32 will be blocked with a picoloyl group using PicoOH and EDCI in DCM, expecting a high yield.

outcome.observation: The remaining free OH was then blocked with a picoloyl group to afford 33 in 97% yield.

evidence.text_span: whose remaining free OH was then blocked with a picoloyl group to afford 33 (97%).

thought.background: Compound 33 has been obtained. The next step is to swap the anomeric MP with an o-cyclopropylethynylbenzoyl group to deliver β-34 exclusively.
thought.gap: This requires oxidative MP removal and dehydrative esterification, with a focus on achieving exclusive β-stereoselectivity.
thought.decision: To deliver β-34 exclusively, 33 will undergo oxidative MP removal (CAN in PhMe/MeCN/H2O), followed by dehydrative esterification with ABzOH (EDCI), expecting a good overall yield and exclusive β-stereoselectivity.

outcome.observation: Swap of the anomeric MP with an o-cyclopropylethynylbenzoyl group was achieved by oxidative MP removal and dehydrative esterification with ABzOH, delivering β-34 exclusively in 60% yield over 2 steps.

evidence.text_span: Then swap of the anomeric MP with an o-cyclopropylethynylbenzoyl group was achieved by oxidative MP removal and dehydrative esterification with ABzOH, delivering b-34 exclusively (60%, 2 steps, Scheme 2B).

thought.background: Synthesis of 3-C-methyl-branched fucosyl o-cyclopropylethynylbenzoate 40 is initiated with 35. The first step is successive benzylation and acid-mediated deisopropylidenation to deliver diol 37 via 36.
thought.gap: This two-step sequence needs to be efficient to prepare diol 37.
thought.decision: To deliver diol 37 via 36, 35 will undergo benzylation (BnBr, NaH in DMF) to give 36, followed by acid-mediated deisopropylidenation (80% HOAc at 80 °C), expecting a good overall yield.

outcome.observation: Successive benzylation and acid-mediated deisopropylidenation delivered diol 37 via 36 in 62% yield over 2 steps.

evidence.text_span: With 35 as the starting material, successive benzylation and acid-mediated deisopropylidenation delivered diol 37 via 36 (62%, 2 steps).

thought.background: Diol 37 has been obtained. The next step is regioselective benzylation of the tertiary C3-OH to afford 38.
thought.gap: Regioselective protection of the tertiary C3-OH is required at elevated temperature.
thought.decision: To afford 38, diol 37 will be subjected to Bu2SnO-mediated regioselective benzylation of the tertiary C3-OH (Bu2SnO, PhMe then BnBr, TBAF, CsF, PhMe, reflux) at elevated temperature, expecting a good yield.

outcome.observation: The diol 37 was then subjected to Bu2SnO-mediated regioselective benzylation of the tertiary C3-OH to afford 38 in 78% yield at elevated temperature.

evidence.text_span: The diol 37 was then subjected to Bu2SnO·-mediated regioselective benzylation of the tertiary C3- OH to afford 38 (78%) at elevated temperature,

thought.background: Compound 38 has been obtained. The next step is to equip it with a picoloyl group under dehydrative conditions to give 39.
thought.gap: Efficient and high-yielding picoloylation is required.
thought.decision: To give 39, 38 will be equipped with a picoloyl group using PicoOH and EDCI in DCM under dehydrative conditions, expecting a high yield.

outcome.observation: Compound 38 was then equipped with a picoloyl group under dehydrative conditions to give 39 in 93% yield.

evidence.text_span: which was then equipped with a picoloyl group under dehydrative conditions to give 39 (93%).

thought.background: Compound 39 has been obtained. The next step is to convert 39 to donor 40 β-stereoselectively.
thought.gap: This requires anomeric hydroxyl group release under oxidative conditions and dehydrative esterification, with a focus on achieving β-stereoselectivity.
thought.decision: To convert 39 to donor 40 β-stereoselectively, it will undergo anomeric hydroxyl group release under oxidative conditions (CAN in MeCN/H2O), followed by dehydrative esterification (ABzOH, EDCI, DMAP, DIPEA in DCM), expecting a good overall yield and β-stereoselectivity.

outcome.observation: Following the sequence of anomeric hydroxyl group release under oxidative conditions and dehydrative esterification, 39 was converted to donor 40 β-stereoselectively in 51% yield over 2 steps.

evidence.text_span: Following the sequence of anomeric hydroxyl group release under oxidative conditions and dehydrative esterification, 39 was converted to donor 40 b-stereoselectively (51%, 2 steps).

thought.background: Donors 34 and 40 have been synthesized, both containing Pico groups, which are expected to facilitate smooth and stereoselective glycosylations via the HAD strategy. Acceptor 31 is also ready.
thought.gap: The coupling of 31 with 34 needs to proceed smoothly and stereoselectively, despite the known inertness of chartarin derivatives as glycosylation acceptors.
thought.decision: To achieve the coupling, 31 will be reacted with 34 under the catalysis of Ph3PAuOTf in DCM with 4A MS, expecting a smooth reaction with moderate to good β-stereoselectivity.

outcome.observation: The coupling of 31 with 34 proceeded smoothly under the catalysis of Ph3PAuOTf, furnishing 41 in 52% yield with moderate to good β-stereoselectivity (α/β = 1:2.8). The β-stereoselectivity was determined from 1H NMR spectra (anomeric proton: 5.46 ppm, d, J = 7.6 Hz for 41β).

evidence.text_span: Indeed, under the catalysis of Ph3PAuOTf.,45 the couplings of 31 with 34 and 40 proceeded smoothly, furnishing 41 and 42 in yields of 52% and 66%, respectively, with moderate to good β·-stereoselectivity (41: α/β = 1:2.8 42: a/β = 1:6.7, Scheme 2C). The β·-stereoselectivity was determined from 1H NMR spectra (anomeric protons: 5.46 ppm, d, J = 7.6 Hz for 41β 5.53 ppm, d, J = 7.8 Hz for 42b).

thought.background: Donors 34 and 40 have been synthesized, both containing Pico groups, which are expected to facilitate smooth and stereoselective glycosylations via the HAD strategy. Acceptor 31 is also ready.
thought.gap: The coupling of 31 with 40 needs to proceed smoothly and stereoselectively, despite the known inertness of chartarin derivatives as glycosylation acceptors.
thought.decision: To achieve the coupling, 31 will be reacted with 40 under the catalysis of Ph3PAuOTf in DCM with 4A MS, expecting a smooth reaction with moderate to good β-stereoselectivity.

outcome.observation: The coupling of 31 with 40 proceeded smoothly under the catalysis of Ph3PAuOTf, furnishing 42 in 66% yield with moderate to good β-stereoselectivity (α/β = 1:6.7). The β-stereoselectivity was determined from 1H NMR spectra (anomeric proton: 5.53 ppm, d, J = 7.8 Hz for 42β).

evidence.text_span: Indeed, under the catalysis of Ph3PAuOTf.,45 the couplings of 31 with 34 and 40 proceeded smoothly, furnishing 41 and 42 in yields of 52% and 66%, respectively, with moderate to good β·-stereoselectivity (41: α/β = 1:2.8 42: a/β = 1:6.7, Scheme 2C). The β·-stereoselectivity was determined from 1H NMR spectra (anomeric protons: 5.46 ppm, d, J = 7.6 Hz for 41β 5.53 ppm, d, J = 7.8 Hz for 42b).

thought.background: Chartarin derivatives are extremely resistant to stereoselective and efficient glycosylation. A series of Yu donors in different protecting patterns were designed, synthesized, and examined.
thought.gap: Despite the success with 34 and 40, other donors, including 20, failed to afford the desired glycosylation product.
thought.decision: To identify viable donors, various protection patterns were explored. However, donors other than 34 and 40 proved ineffective for glycosylation with 31.

outcome.observation: Donors with varied protection patterns, including 20, failed to afford the desired glycosylation product, further verifying the inertness of chartarin derivatives as glycosylation acceptors.

evidence.text_span: Surprisingly, although 34 and 40 could couple with 31, donors with varied protection patterns including 20 failed to afford the desired glycosylation product, further verifying the inertness of chartarin derivatives as glycosylation acceptors (Table S3 in the ESI†).34

thought.background: Previous attempts to glycosylate chartarin acceptor 31 with various Yu donors, including 20, failed, highlighting the inertness of chartarin derivatives. However, donors 34 and 40, which contain C4-OPico and C3-OBn groups, successfully coupled with 31.
thought.gap: Understanding why 34 and 40 were successful while others failed is crucial for future glycosylation strategies.
thought.decision: The successful glycosylation with 34 and 40 implies that C4-OPico and C3-OBn are crucial for successful glycosylation of 31. The less favorable steric hindrance from C2-OBn might be responsible for compromised stereoselectivity in some cases. This insight will guide future donor design.

evidence.text_span: Also, the glycosylation results clearly imply that C4- OPico and C3-OBn are crucial for successful glycosylation of 31 and the less favorable steric hindrance from C2-OBn should be responsible for the compromised stereoselectivity of the glycosylation.

thought.background: The syntheses of chartarin 10-O-monosaccharide glycosides (26, 28, 41, 42) have been accomplished via two different routes. Now, the stage is set for the completion of the synthesis of the four target molecules. The sugar chains of chartreusin derivatives are distinguished by 1,2-connected 1,2-cis intra-sugar-chain glycosidic linkages, requiring well-designed glycosyl donors and a linear tactic.
thought.gap: Stereoselective construction of 1,2-cis glycosidic linkages is challenging. The levulinoyl group has been demonstrated to be powerful in stereocontrol via the remote participation effect.
thought.decision: To achieve 1,2-cis-glycosidic linkage construction, D-fucosyl donors carrying the C4-O-levulinoyl group will be designed. Synthesis of digitalosyl donor 45a will commence with 14, subjected to stannylene acetal-involved regioselective methylation and levulinoylation to provide 44 through intermediate 43.

outcome.observation: Compound 14 was subjected to stannylene acetal-involved regioselective methylation and levulinoylation to provide 44 through intermediate 43 in 82% yield over 2 steps.

evidence.text_span: As the levulinoyl group has been demonstrated to be powerful in stereocontrol of glycosylation reactions via the remote participation effect,46 D-fucosyl donors carrying the C4- O-levulinoyl group were designed to fulfil 1,2-cis-glycosidic linkage construction (Scheme 3). Synthesis of the digitalosyl donor commenced with 14, which was subjected successively to stannylene acetal-involved regioselective methylation and levulinoylation to provide 44 through intermediate 43 (82%, 2 steps).

thought.background: Compound 44 has been obtained. The next step is to transform 44 to donor 45a.
thought.gap: This requires oxidative cleavage of the anomeric MP and subsequent dehydrative esterification of the resulting hemiacetal.
thought.decision: To transform 44 to donor 45a, it will undergo oxidative cleavage of the anomeric MP (CAN in PhMe/MeCN/H2O), followed by dehydrative esterification of the resulting hemiacetal (ABzOH, EDCI, DMAP, DIPEA in DCM), expecting a good overall yield.

outcome.observation: Compound 44 was then transformed to donor 45a through oxidative cleavage of the anomeric MP and the following dehydrative esterification of the resulting hemiacetal in 63% yield over 2 steps.

evidence.text_span: Compound 44 was then transformed to donor 45a through oxidative cleavage of the anomeric MP and the following dehydrative esterification of the resulting hemiacetal (63%, 2 steps, Scheme 3A).

thought.background: Donor 45a has been synthesized. Similarly, donor 45b needs to be prepared from 14.
thought.gap: This requires levulinoylation and anomeric substituent manipulations.
thought.decision: To convert 14 to donor 45b via 46, it will undergo levulinoylation (LevOH, EDCI, DMAP, DIPEA in DCM), followed by anomeric substituent manipulations (CAN in MeCN/H2O, then ABzOH, EDCI, DMAP, DIPEA in DCM), expecting a good overall yield.

outcome.observation: After levulinoylation and anomeric substituent manipulations, 14 was converted to donor 45b via 46 in 66% yield over 3 steps.

evidence.text_span: Similarly, after levulinoylation and anomeric substituent manipulations, 14 was converted to donor 45b via 46 (66%, 3 steps, Scheme 3B).

thought.background: Synthesis of 2-deoxy-2-azido-digitalosyl donor 45c from 12 requires azido group installation first, which is achieved by C2-OH inversion, activation by triflate formation, and substitution with TMSN3. The first step is C2-OH inversion via oxidation under Ley conditions.
thought.gap: Efficient oxidation of 12 to 47 is required.
thought.decision: To achieve C2-OH inversion, 12 will be oxidized under Ley conditions using TPAP and NMO in DCM, expecting a good yield.

outcome.observation: The C2-OH inversion was accomplished by oxidation under Ley conditions, yielding 47 in 82% yield.

evidence.text_span: Synthesis of 2- deoxy-2-azido-digitalosyl donor 45c from 12 required azido group installation first, which was achieved by C2-OH inversion, activation by triflate formation, and substitution with TMSN3 (Scheme 3C). The C2-OH inversion was accomplished by oxidation under Ley conditions (47, 82%)47

thought.background: Compound 47 has been obtained from oxidation. The next step is reduction to provide 48.
thought.gap: Efficient and high-yielding reduction is required.
thought.decision: To provide 48, 47 will be reduced using NaBH4 in MeOH at 0 °C, expecting a good yield.

outcome.observation: Reduction of 47 provided 48 in 88% yield.

evidence.text_span: and reduction to provide 48 (88%).

thought.background: Compound 48 has been obtained. The next step is to install the azido group by triflate formation and substitution with TMSN3.
thought.gap: Efficient and high-yielding azido group installation is required.
thought.decision: To install the azido group, 48 will be triflated (Tf2O, pyridine in DCM at -30 °C), and then the C2-OTf will be substituted with TMSN3 (KF in DMF at rt), expecting a good overall yield.

outcome.observation: After being triflated, the C2-OTf in 48 was subsequently substituted with TMSN3 to finish azido group installation (49) in 90% yield over 2 steps.

evidence.text_span: After being triflated, the C2-OTf in 48 was subsequently substituted with TMSN3 to finish azido group installation (49, 90%, 2 steps).

thought.background: Intermediate 49 has been obtained with the azido group installed. The next step is deacetonation to yield 50.
thought.gap: Efficient and high-yielding deprotection is required.
thought.decision: To yield 50, intermediate 49 will undergo deacetonation using 80% HOAc at 80 °C, expecting a good yield.

outcome.observation: Intermediate 49 then underwent deacetonation to yield 50.

evidence.text_span: Intermediate 49 then underwent deacetonation (50),

thought.background: Compound 50 has been obtained. The next step is regioselective methylation to yield 51.
thought.gap: Efficient and regioselective methylation is required.
thought.decision: To yield 51, 50 will undergo regioselective methylation using Bu2SnO, Mel, and CsF in PhMe/DMF, expecting a high yield.

outcome.observation: Regioselective methylation of 50 yielded 51 in 99% yield.

evidence.text_span: regioselective methylation (51), (99%)

thought.background: Compound 51 has been obtained. The next step is levulinoylation to provide 52.
thought.gap: Efficient and high-yielding levulinoylation is required.
thought.decision: To provide 52, 51 will undergo levulinoylation using LevOH, EDCI, DMAP, and DIPEA in DCM, expecting a high yield.

outcome.observation: Levulinoylation of 51 provided 52 in 97% yield.

evidence.text_span: and levulinoylation to provide 52 (97%, 3 steps).

thought.background: Compound 52 has been obtained. The next step is to obtain the desired donor 45c smoothly as the sole β-isomer.
thought.gap: This requires oxidative MP cleavage and dehydrative acylation sequence, with a focus on achieving exclusive β-stereoselectivity.
thought.decision: To obtain donor 45c, 52 will undergo oxidative MP cleavage (CAN in MeCN/H2O), followed by dehydrative acylation (ABzOH, EDCI, DMAP, DIPEA in DCM), expecting a good overall yield and exclusive β-stereoselectivity.

outcome.observation: The desired donor 45c was obtained smoothly from 52 as the sole β-isomer following the oxidative MP cleavage and dehydrative acylation sequence in 67% yield over 2 steps.

evidence.text_span: Likewise, the desired donor 45c was obtained smoothly from 52 as the sole β-isomer following the oxidative MP cleavage and dehydrative acylation sequence (67%, 2 steps).

thought.background: Donors 45a–c and chartarin 10-O-monosaccharide glycosides are in hand. The stage is set for the completion of the synthesis of the four target molecules. The first target is chartreusin 1. This involves condensation of 45a (donor) with 26 (acceptor).
thought.gap: The condensation needs to proceed uneventfully and α-stereoselectively.
thought.decision: To afford disaccharide glycoside 53, 45a will be condensed with 26 under the catalysis of Ph3PAuOTf, expecting an α-stereoselective reaction.

outcome.observation: With 45a as the donor and 26 as the acceptor, the condensation proceeded uneventfully under the catalysis of Ph3PAuOTf to afford disaccharide glycoside 53 (88%) α-stereoselectively. The configuration of the newly forged glycosidic linkage was assigned according to the corresponding anomeric proton (6.15 ppm, d, J = 2.8 Hz).

evidence.text_span: Expectedly, with 45a as the donor and 26 as the acceptor, the condensation proceeded uneventfully under the catalysis of Ph3PAuOTf to afford disaccharide glycoside 53 (88%) α-stereoselectively. The configuration of the newly forged glycosidic linkage was assigned according to the corresponding anomeric proton (6.15 ppm, d, J = 2.8 Hz).

thought.background: Disaccharide glycoside 53 has been obtained. The next step is to prepare intermediate 54 by desilylation and cleavage of the levulinoyl group.
thought.gap: Efficient and high-yielding deprotection is required.
thought.decision: To yield intermediate 54, 53 will undergo desilylation and cleavage of the levulinoyl group, expecting a good overall yield.

outcome.observation: Desilylation and cleavage of the levulinoyl group yielded intermediate 54 in 88% yield over 2 steps.

evidence.text_span: Desilylation and cleavage of the levulinoyl group yielded intermediate 54 (88%, 2 steps)

thought.background: Intermediate 54 has been obtained. The next step is global deprotection to provide chartreusin 1.
thought.gap: Efficient debenzylation and deacetonation are required.
thought.decision: To provide chartreusin 1, 54 will be exposed to debenzylation/deacetonation conditions, expecting a good overall yield.

outcome.observation: Intermediate 54 was further exposed to debenzylation/deacetonation conditions to provide chartreusin 1 in 76% yield over 2 steps.

evidence.text_span: which was further exposed to debenzylation/deactonation conditions to provide chartreusin 1 (76%, 2 steps).

thought.background: Chartarin monosaccharide glycoside 25 can also serve as a viable acceptor. The next target is D329C 2, which involves coupling 25 with 45b.
thought.gap: The coupling needs to proceed exclusively α-stereoselectively.
thought.decision: To afford 55α exclusively, 25 will be coupled with 45b, expecting an α-stereoselective reaction.

outcome.observation: Chartarin monosaccharide glycoside 25 could also serve as a viable acceptor to couple with 45b, affording 55α (89%) exclusively, as determined by the anomeric proton of the newly incorporated sugar residue (6.14 ppm, d, J = 4.0 Hz).

evidence.text_span: In fact, chartarin monosaccharide glycoside 25 could also serve as a viable acceptor to couple with 45b, affording 55α (89%) exclusively, as determined by the anomeric proton of the newly incorporated sugar residue (6.14 ppm, d, J = 4.0 Hz).

thought.background: Compound 55α has been obtained. The next step is to block the phenolic C6-OH with a Lev group to give 56.
thought.gap: Efficient and high-yielding protection is required.
thought.decision: To give 56, the phenolic C6-OH of 55α will be blocked with a Lev group, expecting a good yield.

outcome.observation: After blocking the phenolic C6-OH with a Lev group, 56 was obtained in 75% yield.

evidence.text_span: After blocking the phenolic C6-OH with a Lev group (56, 75%),

thought.background: Compound 56 has been obtained. The next step is to cleave the benzyl group to deliver disaccharide acceptor 57, primed for further sugar chain elongation.
thought.gap: Efficient and high-yielding deprotection is required.
thought.decision: To deliver disaccharide acceptor 57, the benzyl group in 56 will be cleaved, expecting a good yield.

outcome.observation: The benzyl group in 56 was cleaved to deliver disaccharide acceptor 57 in 82% yield, primed for further sugar chain elongation.

evidence.text_span: the benzyl group in 56 was cleaved to deliver disaccharide acceptor 57 (82%), primed for further sugar chain elongation.

thought.background: Disaccharide acceptor 57 is ready. The next step is glycosylation of 57 with 45a to furnish the fully protected D329C 58.
thought.gap: The glycosylation needs to proceed α-stereoselectively.
thought.decision: To furnish the fully protected D329C 58, glycosylation of 57 with 45a will be performed, expecting an α-stereoselective reaction.

outcome.observation: Glycosylation of 57 with 45a proceeded α-stereoselectively to furnish the fully protected D329C 58 (92%), as verified by H'''-1 which resides at 5.14 ppm (d, J = 3.6 Hz).

evidence.text_span: Again, glycosylation of 57 with 45a proceeded α-stereoselectively to furnish the fully protected D329C 58 (92%), as verified by H'''–1 which resides at 5.14 ppm (d, J = 3.6 Hz).

thought.background: Fully protected D329C 58 has been obtained. The next step is global deprotection to provide D329C 2, the most complex member of chartreusin-type APP glycosides.
thought.gap: Efficient debenzylation, deisopropylidenation, and delevulinoylation are required.
thought.decision: To provide D329C 2, global deprotection of 58 will be accomplished by debenzylation, deisopropylidenation, and delevulinoylation, expecting a good overall yield.

outcome.observation: Global deprotection of 58 was accomplished by debenzylation, deisopropylidenation, and delevulinoylation to provide D329C 2 in 88% yield over 3 steps.

evidence.text_span: Global deprotection of 58 was accomplished by debenzylation, deisopropylidenation, and delevulinoylation to provide D329C 2 (88%, 3 steps), the most complex member of chartreusin-type APP glycosides known thus far.48

thought.background: Synthesis of elsamicin A entails glycosylation of 2-OH of 3-C-methyl-branched fucoside 28 with donor 45c.
thought.gap: This glycosylation might be problematic due to severe steric hindrance, potentially leading to compromised stereoselectivity and efficiency, and partial desilylation.
thought.decision: To afford 59, glycosylation of 28 with 45c will be attempted under optimized conditions (DMF as additive in CF3Ph at 50 °C). Due to anticipated issues, the glycosylation and following desilylation will be conducted continuously without isolation of the intermediate.

outcome.observation: The glycosylation of 28 with 45c suffered from compromised stereoselectivity and efficiency even under optimized conditions, and partial desilylation was observed. The glycosylation and following desilylation were conducted continuously to afford 59 as an easily separable mixture of α/β isomers favoring the desired α isomer (α/β = 4:1) in 52% yield.

evidence.text_span: Indeed, the glycosylation of 28 with 45c suffered from compromised stereoselectivity and efficiency even under the optimized conditions [DMF as additive49 in CF3Ph at 50 °C],34 and partial desilylation was also observed (Table S4 in the ESI†). Thus, the glycosylation step and the following desilylation step were conducted continuously without isolation of the glycosylation product intermediate to afford 59 as an easily separable mixture of α/β isomers favoring the desired α isomer (α/β = 4:1) in 52% yield.

thought.background: Compound 59α has been obtained. The next step is to prepare 60 by delevulinoylation and deisopropylidenation.
thought.gap: Efficient and high-yielding deprotection is required.
thought.decision: To prepare 60, 59α will be subjected to delevulinoylation and deisopropylidenation, expecting a good overall yield.

outcome.observation: Compound 59α was then subjected to delevulinoylation and deisopropylidenation to provide 60 (60%), ready for azido reduction.

evidence.text_span: 59α was then subjected to delevulinoylation and deisopropylidenation to provide 60 (60%) ready for azido reduction.

thought.background: Compound 60 is ready for azido reduction to yield elsamicin A 3. However, azido group reduction can be problematic.
thought.gap: Conventional reduction conditions might not be effective for this substrate.
thought.decision: To reduce the azido group, conventional reduction conditions including Staudinger reduction, Pd/C-mediated hydrogenation, Raney Ni-promoted reduction, and propanedithiol reduction will be attempted.

outcome.observation: The reduction of the azido group proved to be much more difficult than anticipated. Conventional reduction conditions including Staudinger reduction, Pd/C-mediated hydrogenation, Raney Ni-promoted reduction, and propanedithiol reduction could not afford the desired amine product 3.

evidence.text_span: Surprisingly, the reduction of the azido group proved to be much more difficult than what was anticipated, and conventional reduction conditions including Staudinger reduction, Pd/C-mediated hydrogenation, Raney Ni-promoted reduction, and propanedithiol reduction could not afford the desired amine product 3 (Table S5 in the ESI†).34

thought.background: Conventional methods for azido group reduction failed to yield elsamicin A 3 from 60.
thought.gap: A more effective reduction method is needed for this challenging substrate.
thought.decision: To efficiently effect the reduction, the combination of Zn/CuSO4/HOAc will be employed.

evidence.text_span: Eventually, it was found that the combination of Zn/CuSO4/HOAc could efficiently effect the reduction, thus affording elsamicin A 3 successfully (83%).

thought.background: Conventional methods for azido group reduction failed. A new method using Zn/CuSO4/HOAc was identified as effective.
thought.gap: Efficient reduction of the azido group in 60 to afford elsamicin A 3 is required.
thought.decision: To afford elsamicin A 3, 60 will be treated with Zn/CuSO4/HOAc, expecting an efficient reduction.

outcome.observation: The combination of Zn/CuSO4/HOAc could efficiently effect the reduction, thus affording elsamicin A 3 successfully in 83% yield.

evidence.text_span: Eventually, it was found that the combination of Zn/CuSO4/HOAc could efficiently effect the reduction, thus affording elsamicin A 3 successfully (83%).

thought.background: Elsamicin A 3 has been successfully synthesized. For thorough chemical structure determination and comparison with reported data, 3 needs to be acetylated.
thought.gap: Acetylation is required to produce 61 for comparison.
thought.decision: To produce 61, 3 will be acetylated, expecting a good yield.

outcome.observation: For thorough chemical structure determination by comparison with reported data, 3 was acetylated to produce 61 in 85% yield.

evidence.text_span: For thorough chemical structure determination by comparison with reported data, 3 was acetylated to produce 61 (85%).50

thought.background: Elsamicin A 3 has been synthesized. The final target is elsamicin B 4.
thought.gap: Elsamicin B 4 can be accomplished by deisopropylidenation of 27.
thought.decision: To accomplish the synthesis of elsamicin B 4, 27 will undergo deisopropylidenation, expecting a good yield.

outcome.observation: Synthesis of elsamicin B 4 was accomplished by deisopropylidenation of 27 in 84% yield.

evidence.text_span: Finally, synthesis of elsamicin B 4 was accomplished by deisopropylidenation of 27 (84%).

Overall Yield (Chartreusin 1).interpretation: Overall yield for Chartreusin 1 starting from commercially available 5.

Overall Yield (D329C 2).interpretation: Overall yield for D329C 2 starting from commercially available 5.

Overall Yield (Elsamicin A 3).interpretation: Overall yield for Elsamicin A 3 starting from easily available 12.

validation.significance: The establishment of this chemical synthesis strategy marks the first successful total synthesis of chartreusin derivatives, including chartreusin, D329C, and elsamicins A and B. It provides robust and efficient access to these complex natural products, overcoming limitations of previous biosynthesis and chemobiosynthesis methods. The strategy features two complementary routes for key intermediates and addresses challenging glycosidic linkage constructions. Furthermore, the synthesized compounds were used for bioactivity investigations, revealing profound cytotoxic effects against human cancer cell lines and disclosing sugar chain-determined functional mechanisms, which could accelerate the identification of chartreusin derivatives with improved drugability.

source.title: Collective total synthesis of chartreusin derivatives and bioactivity investigations.
source.authors: Yi HZ, Liang SM, Li JJ, Liu H, Liao JX, Liu DY, Zhang QJ, Cai MZ, Sun JS.
